content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What Tom Hanks movie should I watch for T-Hanks Giving?

**Score:** 77
**Body:** Every year I watch a Tom Hanks movie as a tradition since my wife hasn't seen many movies. So far we've watched

"Cast Away"

"Catch Me if You Can"

"A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood"

"Forrest Gump"

"Saving Private Ryan"

"Big"

"Toy Story Marathon"

"You've got Mail"

Edit: Wow. I'm thankful for all these answers! This should last us the next 20 years. Whichever one is at the top when my wife comes home will be the selection. Happy T-Hanks Giving to you all and yours!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Joe Versus the Volcano 

It's one of my favorites from back in the day.
2. The 'Burbs(1989)
3. You don’t even have Apollo 13 listed …
4. Splash(1984) the movie that made him a star
5. The Money Pit
Bachelor Party
6. Splash or Big
7. You've Got Mail -- romance,  holiday meals, misunderstandings, witty dialogue, serious themes of fibding thecright person, all in the Upper West Side

Or, 'That Thing You Do' -- fun, nostalgic, 1960s cars, music, romance.
8. The Terminal
9. Money Pit!
10. A League of Their Own

## 2. Creepy serial killer thrillers like Se7en or True Detective

**Score:** 58
**Body:** As the title says, I'm looking for creepy movies with a grizzly focus on a series of murders. I'm not looking for outright gore like Saw. Se7en and True Detective S01 I think describe the vibe pretty well.

Prisoners usually comes up when I'm looking for movies like Se7en, and it's a fantastic movie, but I'm looking for slightly higher creep/murder factor.

Preferably movies, but I'll gladly take TV show suggestions as well.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If you don’t mind foreign these are among the best

Cure (1997)

Memories of Murder (2003)
  - Memories of Murder might be my favorite murder mystery ever. It keeps me going down the rabbit hole of the news stories behind the case. It's really crazy.
2. The Girl With The Dragon Tattoo
3. Silence of the Lambs comes to mind...
4. Manhunter (1986) is a good, older movie.
5. Mindhunter, which is a series, is the only other one that I consider to be of the same caliber as Seven and True Detective S1 when it comes to this genre, even if we only get two seasons of it. Also made by David Fincher who made Seven.
6. Try Frailty with Bill Paxton and Matthew McConaughey
7. Memories of Murder is at the top
8. Copycat (1995)
9. If you don't mind subtitles I'd watch "I Saw the Devil" (2010) 

I couldn't stop thinking about it for awhile when I first watched it, similar to the first time I watched Seven

## 3. The Descendants (2011)

**Score:** 52
**Body:** This one hit me way harder than I expected. George Clooney running in flip-flops. That image alone is almost a perfect summary of the whole movie - a man trying to deal with a huge crisis while completely unprepared and dressed for the wrong life. It's funny, brutal, and feels incredibly real.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yeah this one doesn’t get the love it deserves. It feels a bit forgotten and that’s a shame. This and Up in the Air make for a great Clooney twin billing.
  - That's a perfect double feature. Both are about men defined by their control (or lack of it) over their own lives, just in completely different worlds.
  - That's a solid double feature right there. Both movies have that same vibe of dudes just trying to figure out life when everything goes sideways. Plus Clooney actually acts in these instead of just being charming George Clooney
2. Really liked this one too.  His dress is typical of Hawaii.  Official business attire for men is an aloha shirt.  Only lawyers wear suits.  God I miss that place.
3. This movie was written by Jim Rash, who plays Dean Pelton on Community!
4. Excellent movie.
  - This movie excells in dealing with loss and grief but my favirites are the scenes w the kids which bring some much needed humor and pathoa
5. Excellent movie!!!
6. This movie was one of the ones I had one repeat during my separation and divorce and my whole life was falling apart.  I don't watch it as much now because its so tied to those emotions and events but damn George knew what I was going through.
7. It actually is my favorite Clooney movie. It’s poignant, funny and has an excellent cast. Hawaii is so beautiful.

## 4. Any Netflix docs about data breaches or identity theft worth watching

**Score:** 51
**Body:** I have been on a documentary kick lately and wanted something that covers real world data breaches or identity theft cases. Not the dramatized stuff, more like how these things actually happen in the background and how people end up getting hit with fraud.  
  
I searched around but Netflix tends to bury half their documentary catalog unless it is trending. If anyone has watched something good on this topic there, I am open to anything. True crime style, interviews with victims, behind the scenes investigative stuff, whatever.  
I am mostly looking for something that explains how personal info gets stolen or leaked without the usual TV style exaggeration.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [The Great Hack](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/566222-the-great-hack) (2019), on Netflix in the US.  
>Data—arguably the world’s most valuable asset—is being weaponized to wage cultural and political wars. The dark world of data exploitation is uncovered through the unpredictable, personal journeys of players on different sides of the explosive Cambridge Analytica/Facebook data story.  
  
[Zero Days](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/380808-zero-days) (2016), on Prime in the US.  
>Alex Gibney explores the phenomenon of Stuxnet, a self-replicating computer virus discovered in 2010 by international IT experts. Evidently commissioned by the US and Israeli governments, this malware was designed to specifically sabotage Iran’s nuclear programme. However, the complex computer worm ended up not only infecting its intended target but also spreading uncontrollably.
  - Came here to say "the great hack" aswell... Op look up "Web of Make Believe …" too
2. I think you would enjoy CyberBunker. It follows the exploits of a datacenter built inside a WW2 era German bunker which catered to the worst cybercriminals and dark web denizens. It catalogs their rise and downfall.
3. ***the social dilemma*** is definitely worth watching, and i also liked ***can i tell you a secret?*** as well as ***terms & conditions may apply.***  
there's also a docuseries called ***dirty money*** with two seasons that include the themes you listed !!
  - Dirty Money is so good, that Equifax episode was wild. The Social Dilemma gets a bit preachy but still solid for understanding how your data gets harvested in the first place
4. CitizenFour (featuring Edward Snowden).
5. Have you seen The Imposter (2012) ? Cause that just kept getting crazier
6. The Ashley Madison miniseries on Netflix was a great watch

## 5. Looking for Movies With a Truly Mind Blowing Plot Twist

**Score:** 52
**Body:** I’d love some movie recommendations with a truly mind blowing plot twist. Not something predictable or cliché. I’m looking for a twist that really surprises me and keeps me thinking long after the movie ends. and please, not the typical or super well known ones everyone always recommends

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Game (1997)
2. The Prestige is one of my favorites, but also Memento. Both directed by Christopher Nolan.
3. Primal Fear
4. Fallen (1998)
5. The twist in the movie r/MovieSuggestions where the same question gets asked twice a week phrased slightly differently, and it turns out the sub is trapped in a time loop, doomed to answer the exact same question with the exact same answers over and over for all eternity.
6. Incendies (2010)
7. The Prestige
8. Vanilla Sky
9. Adaptation but watch Being Jon Malkovich first.
10. The Others

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Despite not making a film in over 30 years gonzo Hong Kong director Lam Ngai Kai is a experiencing something of a small resurgence with the help of physical media

**Score:** 31
**Body:** US audiences are probably most familiar with him from his hyper violent manga adaptation Riki-Oh: The Story of Ricky (1991). A head smashing clip from that film was for many years featured on the early Daily Show episodes before Five Questions.

Lam started with Shaw Bros. Studios in the 1970's & established himself as a cinematographer. In 1981 he directed his first film One Way Only. It was a movie about illegal street races co-directed with The Killer star Danny Lee.

His style really came to fruition with The Seventh Curse (1986). It's an absolutely insane martial arts horror spectacle. In the movie a doctor/adventurer relays the tale of his most recent exploits. Dr. Yuen Chen-hsieh visited Thailand a year earlier & ran afoul of a Dark Sorcerer that placed a blood curse on him.

Chow Yun-fat gets top billing but don't get fooled as it's a glorified cameo. The film doesn't suffer though from his lack of presence. The plot makes sense even though it contains a fair amount of mystical mumbo-jumbo. It mostly careens from one action set piece to another.

You have a hostage shootout with the cops, a monk battle, a skeleton fight, a demon fight & so much more. As I mentioned in the subject there's some nasty practical effects. The initial outbreak of the Dr's blood curse complete with pulsating veins is well done. There's also a nifty demon transformation scene & the sorcerer controls a nasty little monster he throws at people.

He directed eight more films over the next six years. Most of which had the same level of bonkers energy combined with wild action & over the top gore. Then in 1992 he stopped directing for reasons that don't seem documented.

He'd had releases on DVD & Blu-ray before. Within the last year though many are getting cleaned up & being made available for the first time in decades. People are discovering or re-discovery the madman magic in his films.

I'd love to know more about him & why he stepped away. He seems like he could be an interesting documentary subject based on his time spent in HK during many years of their golden age.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 2. Park Chan-wook's No Other Choice (2025) - One of the better constructed films of this year

**Score:** 26
**Body:** > One of the better-constructed films of 2025, with both the subject matter and the technical form working in harmony to deliver a black comedy-directed attack on late-stage capitalism and the critical desperation of the middle class to stabilize the status quo. “No other choice” is a morality override phrase to maintain your family as a unit and to mitigate the fear of downward mobility. The irony of a fully-automated factory creating paper.

> Man-su gets away scot-free after committing murders almost through divine intervention, as one-in-a-million coincidences conveniently tie up every loose end and everything falls into place to keep the machinery running. The centripetal zooms, the thematic linking of two shots, the mechanical crane shots, the surreal 'Honey I Shrunk the kids' shot towards the end. The ridiculous song playing over the first murder sequence is hilarious. All of it is part of Park's wacky societal farce.

His previous film, Decision to Leave (2022) and others before it have displayed DePalman and Hitchcockian instincts and his modulation of tone is so precise and almost like those two greats, especially in this film.

I'm still unable to grasp his metaphors with the all the vegetation, the constant presence of trees and plants, and the surreal aspects especially [this shot!](https://media3.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExdWZlZ2Z3MDE1ZjAxNmZ0a21rbWFpdm15OXJjNGQ5ZGNjY3J6YjMyNiZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/6qlulDzOv4pQF6gWXD/giphy.gif)

Can someone help me with this?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I disagree. I think it is much better visually than script-wise. It was a mess and a half for two-thirds of the film, where it really just wants to be this fun film about someone who is about to make big 'choices'. It tries to mash this surface level talks about gender roles (which it ultimately buckles away in the better last frame), man vs machine and this 'choice' plot. Like, everything about this film is too convenient storytelling. What do you mean the police did not even try preliminary police work in the first house? Everything about the two 'choices' was convoluted happenstance.

It's very disappointing.
  - I felt the same way. 

*My review from last week:*

**NO OTHER CHOICE** (2025), my 4th, highly -praised drama by Park Chan-wook. This is a remake of the Costa-Gavras 2005 thriller 'The Ax’, which was also based on a Donald E. Westlake novel. The premise was intriguing: A senior manger at a large paper company is laid off, and since he can’t be hired again, he starts killing the few selected candidates who apply for the same high-level job that he does.

It has 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes, but I was disappointed and bored with it. While the dark political French thriller could intelligently criticise both capitalism and the bourgeois, and still deliver a compelling story, this (long) version was a light comedy that missed that or any other mark.
    - Now, I need to watch that.

## 3. Extremely excited for Resurrection by Bi Gan, but how do I “get” movies like this when it’s non-traditional in its structure of the story?

**Score:** 9
**Body:** Sorry if this is a stupid question, I’ve only recently become more interested in watching different types of movies and from what I hear this movie feels like I’m going to be confused as hell. The most unique thing I did movie wise is take a movie history class in high school which went over some classics that we watched (Godfather, citizen Kane etc)

But, First off this movie looks incredible from the trailers. From what I see on reviews, is this is a homage to cinemas in general which I feel is probably gonna blow over my head for having not really watched THAT much movies. And my main concern with the plot And usually the plot line and whatnot is relatively easy to follow unlike this from what I hear. 

Only recently I got A list and even then I’ve watched  mainly popular things with the exception of some anime films that are not popular in the states lol. 

Should I just watch the movie and not worry about trying to figure out the plot just from it being more dream like and just “feel” the movie, whatever that feeling is? 



### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Being able to “vibe” with a movie is definitely a skill you develop but it also depends if it’s a movie on your wavelength. For instance, Terrence Malick works very well for me, The Sound of Falling did not. Resurrection is one of my favorites of the year, and very easy to just let go and absorb. Another thing that makes Resurrection easier is it’s essentially broken up into 5 different short films, so if one isn’t working for you, just hold out a little bit lol. It will change to something else shortly. 

Go try some Malick - watch The Thin Red Line. I think that’s a good entry point for movies like this. It’s really just about sitting there and letting the movie take you for the ride.
2. I think it’s a matter of getting reps in, and realizing that “getting it” just really means “enjoying it” a lot of the time. I think Bi Gan shares a lot with Lynch tbh, so I’d try giving films like Mulholland Drive and Eraserhead a spin. These are films where there aren’t *really* spoilers, so if you’re actually concerned, it might be worth it to read a plot synopsis before hand. That way you don’t have to be in your head about “not getting it.” It took me having seen a few films from a few of these types of directors before it really clicked. 

Think of them as poetry rather than novels—it’s an exploration of a theme or situation with the focus on how to evoke certain emotions through imagery and sound, rather than plot. And sometimes the amazement comes from realizing these dreamlike, illogical approaches can actually do a better job at getting to the emotional core of a story than the actual story itself ever could. 

I’m a big fan of Bi Gan, but I know people who despise his stuff. It’s not for everyone. I highly, highly recommend Long Days Journey into night though.
  - Love this description of interpreting these kinds of films as poetry.


Are you a fan of Tarkovsky's Stalker?


That's probably the most challenging film I ever sat through and that left me wondering if Resurrection might not be for me lol. I've been waffling on whether to add it to my watchlist, but might do it with this new perspective in mind!
    - Yeah I like Stalker. I’m not the biggest Tarkovsky fan to be honest though-only seen that and Ivan’s Childhood, and found both good but not mind-blowing. I respect his work but there’s a lot of ‘slow cinema’ filmmakers I’d personally take over him any day. Bela Tarr, Hou Hsiao Hsien, Joe, Tsai Ming Liang, Kelly Reinhardt, for example. 

But outside of maybe Joe and occasionally Hou I think most of their work, including Tarkovsky, actually falls in a different (though still related) camp from the likes of Lynch, Bi Gan, and Malick. Still poetic and meandering, but not quite as disconnected from ‘reality’, or at least traditional representations of reality.
    - Bi Gan is an enormous fan of Tarkovsky. His first two films were almost too evocative of his work.
3. Watch it and see what you take from it. Don't confuse 'confusing' with profound, either. Just because something is artsy doesn't make it better than some other film, its in its delivery, its structure, its overt and subtle symbolism and transcendent metaphors. 

What I'm trying to say is, be a student of film, but that doesn't' mean you become an uncritical 'fanboy'.
4. The structure is not that complicated in the first place. It's like Holy Motors, but with a different narrative trick to get you through the different stories. 

Bresson said: "I'd rather people feel a film before understanding it." 

You don't need to understand it to like it. I didn't think the film was inaccessible, as it's several small stories so even if you don't like one, you might like the next one.
5. It's a very well made film and I think you'll enjoy it regardless. 

From what I understand, it's more of a homage to the history of Chinese cinema specifically. I'm only really familiar with the Chinese cinema of the past 15 years or so (and even then, I have very limited knowledge). But I still really enjoyed Resurrection.

Without spoiling it, although it has a non-traditional structure, it's very easy to follow after the initial set up.

## 4. What if they had gone with the original idea for the intro to Once Upon a Time in the West?

**Score:** 8
**Body:** The plan was to have three main characters (Eastwood, Van Cleef, Wallach) from The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly be ambushed and killed by Harmonica in the opening scene. I guess Eastwood didn't want to do it. 

What if this actually had been the opening scene and how would have audiences reacted? It seems like it might have angered a lot of people who enjoyed the Dollars Trilogy, but maybe not.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There was a certain sense of “cheekiness” to the whole Dollars trilogy that kind of fit the tone of that proposed scene well, honestly. I feel that the intro would have worked as an example of that, but also because it would have helped herald a shift away from that tone after - as Once Upon a Time, to me, feels far more grounded and less “cheeky” than any of the Dollars films. If anything, that difference in tone means the lack of that scene is probably for the best. 

That said, the version of the scene that ended up in the film might be one of my all time favorite openers. The western in medias res stripped to its bare essentials and drawn out to a spinetingling extreme.
  - I know exactly what you mean, but the thing is, Leone always had a sense of self-referential artifice to all his movies, including OUATITW. That whole opening scene is so drawn out as to draw attention to itself anyway, and I personally think that having three recognisable faces would create even more anticipation and make their deaths even more impactful.

Let’s not forget that Henry Fonda was basically stunt casting in the film too… at the time he was known only for playing honorable, good men. The sweeping, attention-drawing camera move that introduces him to the audience in this is no more distracting than having Eastwood, Wallach and Van Cleef at the start.
    - Hard disagree. Once Upon a Time In the West works even more because it's separate from the previous movies for me. Having Clint Eastwood in there would've been really distracting and would've taken away from what the movie was trying to achieve, imo. Yeah, it's a similar setting, but it's a different story. And that's what makes it great.
  - The most  exciting  opening scene, credit sequence and introduction of the hero I can think of: the setting, camera shots, the villains, the wait,  the silence , the wind, the coats, the train,  the hero, the shootout, the music: Exhilarating . It's so good,  that any  alternative introduction sounds like a bad, ugly idea in comparison. As for casting  Jack Elam, Woody Strode and Al Mullock are perfect.  I didn't know Leone had thought of an alternative scene.  Did you know Mullock committed suicide right  after shooting it?
2. Let’s be clear here: Leone wanted to have the three actors play the roles, but they wouldn’t be the actual characters from The Good The Bad and The Ugly (especially since, you know, one’s already dead).

It was just going to be a fun nod. No one was going to get angry at some self-referential stunt casting, especially since fans of the Dollars films were already used to Eastwood being cast as three different very similar characters.
3. I do not think this was ever seriously considered. Perhaps Leone said that jokingly as there are three hit men but I would rather think it was something made up many years later.  I am also not sure it would have been a good beginning anyway and could cause backlash when you see the hero from three movies being shot at the start.
4. I don’t know about angered them, The Good The Bad and The Ugly was the film with the “If you’re gonna shoot, shoot, don’t talk” scene after all. I feel like if anything that kind of gallows humor would have resonated more with the target audience. They would have rightfully recognized it as a gag. See also Han shooting first and Indy shooting the swordsmen.

I still haven’t seen For a Few Dollars More or Once Upon a Time in the West. The trick is finding time to myself to watch them as my wife hates Westerns. Aside from Tombstone (Val Kilmer with that terminal illness drip).
  - For a few dollars more is incredible. Please make time to watch it. 

## 5. Do you think Train Dreams really needed a voiceover?

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I saw Train Dreams last night, and enjoyed it. It has a lovely atmosphere of melancholy, and would pair well with other Pacific Northwest films such as Pig, or Leave No Trace. 

But did it really need that voiceover? I feel that without it, I would have understood what was going on just as well, but there would have been more room for my own interpretations to connect the dots. I would have liked the film to demand a little bit more from me.

Conventional wisdom seems to be that voiceovers are best avoided, as they’re a symptom of telling, rather than showing. Sometimes the voiceover provides a counterpoint to what’s happening on screen, in which case they can be effective. But Train Dreams isn’t doing that, it’s a fairly conventional voiceover that explains what’s happening and what the protagonist is thinking. Why do you think they decided to include one? I don’t know if the film was made by Netflix or just picked up by them, so a really hope it isn’t another example of Netflix spoon-feeding their audience, so they can half-watch while they’re on their phones. 

How do you feel about voiceovers in general, and in Train Dreams in particular? All opinions welcome!



### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There are details about the guy's life that wouldn't have come across without the voiceover. We wouldn't have known he was an orphan, what his feelings about some of his different co-workers were, his thoughts at the end, etc. It would have been much vaguer. 

It's not an action film, it's primarily about the guy's thoughts on his life, so it merited the voiceover in my view. 

It is based on a novella, so I presume they used voiceover directly from that.
2. I think ultimately the voiceover ‘was’ the film. The voice of the narrator and that particular wistful storytelling manner was baked in from the start and how they wanted to deliver this film. Without it, it would radically change the film and what it is from a kind of melancholic musing on the passing of time to a much more arthouse film about a lonely man living in solitude. For me personally, the film wouldn’t have been half as moving without some of those quotes.

It’s an adaptation of a Denis Johnson novel, so I don’t think it’s necessarily ‘telling rather than showing’ so much as the director being drawn to the story because of the original author’s voice like the adaptation of his novel Jesus’ Son. He has a very particular manner of writing that the film wants to preserve by using large amounts of his text as narration. It wasn’t put there by Netflix, it’s an artistic decision.

It’s ultimately a film about how we are all just a small piece of an ever changing landscape so it’s crucial that we hear the story of how the bridge became useless a few years later for example. The film would probably work without it but it’s a radically different experience.
  - My issue taking again the example of the bridge is it could have been cinematically explained to the audience, a cut to that same location later on with the modernized bridge. Instead you have some guy telling you about it, which begs the question of why is this a film at all and not still just a story?
    - >it could have been cinematically explained to the audience, a cut to that same location later on with the modernized bridge. 

That wouldn't explain that the new bridge was built just 10 years later, making the construction effort largely redundant, unless you added subtitles to explain this, which is hardly any more "cinematic". 

There is a lot about this story that would not be expressed just using visuals. Imagine those scenes at the end when he's in the airplane or visiting the bazaar, or how he felt taxi-ing the young women. We only know what he's thinking because of the voiceover. 

You could possibly devise other scenes to get the same message across without the narration - but then it wouldn't really be an adaptation of the novel. 

VO is perfectly valid. There's no law that cinema must be exclusively visual or diegetic, or that we can't peep into character's minds from time to time. 

Why is having a film score more "cinematic"?

The VO also probably served the function of fan service, letting enjoyers of the book hear the author's voice come through. 

>begs the question of why is this a film at all and not still just a story

Were you going to read this obscure story? No. Will you watch what's on Netflix? Yes. Question answered.
      - It would be like removing the voiceover from Sunset Boulevard or The Big Lebowski. Yes the story would still make sense but you’d be losing all the character and richness that make them classic.

The entire film is summed up by that closing narration. Without it, it would just be an old man goes up in a plane and smiles a bit.
      - I’m not saying voice over is always unneeded. I could name many examples of where I think it contributes to a film, but in this one it’s used as a crutch, or if I’m being cynical a means to keep audiences engaged while at home doing other things. It is Netflix after all.

The bridge scene even shows him ten years older, looking at the new bridge, so it explains the meaning in both ways, showing you as well as simply laying it out plainly.

Also you’re saying adapting a story replaces the need to read it?
3. I understand *why* the VO was preserved the way it was. It not only lifted sections directly from the novella, but it’s even the same actor who did the audiobook way back when, Will Patton.

But I wasn’t a fan. Don’t get me wrong, I love Will Patton, especially now that he is in his gravelly old man phase of his career. But the narration felt hollow to me. Felt like it was just trying to tell me what to feel. 

I thought a lot about *Tree of Life* and *Days of Heaven* and how those movies had narration that felt truly ethereal and mystical, somewhere between a character’s internal monologue and some distant recounting of a memory. 

I thought a lot about *The Assassination of Jesse James*, where Hugh Ross’ fabulous narration has a dispassionate, almost documentary-esque feel to it, which adds texture and detail without feeling manipulative. 

*Train Dreams*’ narration felt like it was trying to straddle the line between these, leaning more towards the latter. But I just think Will Patton puts too much sauce on it. It felt distracting and yet, not as emotionally impactful as I think it should have.
4. >There are details about the guy's life that wouldn't have come across without the voiceover. 



>It’s an adaptation of a Denis Johnson novel, so I don’t think it’s necessarily ‘telling rather than showing’ so much as the director being drawn to the story because of the original author’s voice

  
This shouldn't be a hot take, but if you need VO to convey a character's inner thoughts or mimic the voice of the book it was adapted from, you are a bad filmmaker.

The VO is a symptom of Bentley failing to understand what makes Train Dreams interesting, as further evidenced by him re-weighting the story to make Robert nothing more than "a guy with a dead wife" rather than the far more interesting version he is in the book, and moving the definitive opening scene of the book to later on in the story, rewriting it to make Robert explicitly not racist. VO is used as a crutch because he knows no other way to convey characterization. I detest cheap sentiment
5. I couldn’t help noticing while watching it that most all the movie could be told both without voiceover or actually any dialogue at all. There were many sequences that felt too spelled out and on the nose, where a little subtlety could’ve gone a long way

---
